gm.name: Jon-Eric Sullivan
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Vice President of Player Personnel, Green Bay Packers

career_path.role: Football Operations Department / NFS Combine Representative / SW Region Senior Evaluator

career_path.mentor: Ted Thompson (2016), Brian Gutekunst (peer, became GM 2018)

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ted Thompson (philosophical foundation) and Brian Gutekunst (day-to-day collaboration for 22 years)
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Ron Wolf → Ted Thompson → Brian Gutekunst → Jon-Eric Sullivan. This is one of the most direct and prestigious scouting lineages in the NFL. Ron Wolf built the modern Packers scouting infrastructure in the 1990s. Ted Thompson codified it into a 'draft-and-develop' philosophy with extreme discipline. Gutekunst loosened the reins slightly on free agency and trades while maintaining the draft-first DNA. Sullivan internalized all of this across 22 years. The Wolf/Thompson tree has produced John Dorsey, John Schneider, Reggie McKenzie, Alonzo Highsmith, and Eliot Wolf as GMs or de facto GMs — all sharing a core belief in athletic traits, positional versatility, and building through the draft. Sullivan explicitly credits Thompson as his guiding influence and called him out by name in his introductory presser.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Sullivan has publicly described free agency as 'dangerous waters,' echoing Thompson more than Gutekunst (who became a more willing free agency spender). Sullivan appears to lean closer to Thompson's draft-only purity than Gutekunst's hybrid approach. He has also explicitly stated he wants to build 'from the inside out' with OL/DL priority — a departure from the recent Packers tendency to draft WRs and skill-position athletes early. His hire of Kyle Smith (Washington/Atlanta tree, A.J. Smith lineage) as assistant GM introduces a secondary scouting influence that is more aggressive on defensive line investment.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Zero drafts of his own data. The Packers scouting tree IS the profile. Sullivan's 22 years in Green Bay, including direct involvement as director of college scouting (2016-2017) and VP of player personnel (2022-2025), mean the Gutekunst-era draft picks are substantially his fingerprints as well. Will drop to 0.50 after 2026 draft, 0.25 after 2027 draft.

key_influencers.name: Jeff Hafley
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Defensive-minded HC, DB coach by trade. Coached Darrelle Revis at Pittsburgh. Ran 4-3 single-high safety defense with heavy Cover-3 and simulated pressures in Green Bay. Values press-man corners, penetrating DL, and rangy single-high safeties. Overlapped with Sullivan in Green Bay for two seasons (2024-2025). Will have significant input on defensive personnel.

key_influencers.name: Kyle Smith
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Son of former Chargers GM A.J. Smith. Spent 11 years in Washington (2010-2020), rising to VP of Player Personnel. Then 5 years with Atlanta Falcons as VP/assistant GM (2021-2025). Washington tenure emphasized heavy DL investment (Jonathan Allen, Da'Ron Payne, Montez Sweat, Chase Young). Atlanta tenure included Kyle Pitts, Drake London, Bijan Robinson, Michael Penix Jr. Known as a strong evaluator who was considered 'the brains of the operation' in Atlanta by some reports.

key_influencers.name: Jon Robinson
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Executive
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Titans GM (2016-2022). Has connection to Hafley from 2013 Buccaneers. Known for aggressive roster moves (traded for Tannehill, traded A.J. Brown). Drafted Malik Willis. Adds a more aggressive, trade-willing perspective to the front office.

key_influencers.name: Champ Kelly
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Executive
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Bears pro scouting director and Raiders assistant GM. Served as Dolphins interim GM in late 2025. Returned in a senior advisory role. Replaced Reggie McKenzie (another Packers tree executive). Provides institutional knowledge of current Dolphins roster.

key_influencers.name: Sean Duggan
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator
key_influencers.known_biases: Brought from Green Bay with Hafley. Will run the same 4-3 single-high scheme. Defensive personnel decisions will be heavily influenced by scheme requirements.

front_office.power_dynamics: Sullivan has clear final say. He and Hafley were hired as a paired duo from Green Bay with shared vision. Owner Stephen Ross described wanting 'comprehensive change' and appears to be giving Sullivan significant autonomy. Kyle Smith adds a strong second opinion from a different scouting tree (Washington/Atlanta). Robinson provides veteran GM experience. This is a collaborative but GM-led structure.
front_office.owner_interference: medium — Ross has historically been involved (fired Grier mid-season, was 'a big believer in McDaniel' for a time, brought in Troy Aikman as consultant during GM search). However, the comprehensive overhaul and Sullivan's mandate suggest Ross is granting more autonomy to this regime than previous ones.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: BPA with a team-value approach. Sullivan: 'You really have no idea what your needs are going to be. As we build a board, subconsciously, with a team-value approach, you'll stack it for need.' Also: 'Build from the inside out — dominant on the OL, dominant on the DL.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Inferred from Packers history: Gutekunst's first-round picks skewed heavily defensive (7 of 9) and toward premium positions (EDGE, CB, QB, OT, LB). The Packers under Gutekunst/Sullivan were willing to take the best athlete available even at non-traditional premium positions (LB Quay Walker at 22, S Darnell Savage at 21). Sullivan's stated emphasis on building inside-out (OL/DL) suggests he may lean more need-driven at the line of scrimmage than Gutekunst was, while still following BPA at skill positions. The Packers frequently took BPA even when it created positional surplus (3 safeties in 2024, 2 WRs in 2025).
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Sullivan claims BPA but also explicitly states 'build from the inside out' and OL/DL dominance. In a rebuild year with needs everywhere, BPA and need should frequently align, making this mismatch less relevant in 2026. Watch for whether he takes a premium-position BPA (e.g., CB at 11) or reaches for an OL/DL if one isn't the clear best player.

trade_triggers: Player they've targeted falling within striking distance (Gutekunst traded up for Jordan Love in 2020, for Jaire Alexander in 2018, for Lukas Van Ness in 2023)
Accumulating Day 2 picks to address multiple needs (Packers frequently traded back in rounds 2-3 to gain extra picks)
Clear tier break on board where next cluster of players is 8+ picks away
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Sullivan has explicitly vowed to 'build through the draft' and called free agency 'dangerous waters.' The Packers under Gutekunst/Sullivan stockpiled picks regularly (11+ picks in 4 of 8 drafts). Expect Sullivan to be aggressive about accumulating picks, especially in a rebuild year. He is unlikely to trade future first-round picks given his stated philosophy and the cautionary tale of the Grier regime trading away picks for Tyreek Hill and Bradley Chubb.

position_avoidance: RB in round 1 — Packers have never taken a RB in round 1 under Gutekunst/Sullivan. Sullivan's 'inside out' philosophy deprioritizes skill positions early. WR in round 1 was avoided for 23 years until 2025 (Matthew Golden).
position_premium: EDGE and CB — Packers drafted 2 EDGEs (Gary R1, Van Ness R1) and 2 CBs (Alexander R1, Stokes R1) in first round under Gutekunst. DL — Sullivan explicitly stated 'dominant on the DL' and the Packers drafted Kenny Clark R1 and Devonte Wyatt R1. OL — Sullivan explicitly stated 'dominant on the OL' and the Packers invested heavily (Jenkins R2, Morgan R1, plus 3 OL in 2022 draft).

EDGE.archetype_summary: Elite-athlete, high-ceiling pass rusher with length and explosiveness who can be developed. Packers EDGE picks under this regime have prioritized raw athleticism and physical tools over polish or college production. Think Rashan Gary (9.95 RAS, 12th overall, modest college sack numbers) and Lukas Van Ness (versatile, could play inside or outside). The regime is willing to bet on physical traits and develop technique.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33 inches preferred based on Gary (33.5 in) and Van Ness (33.25 in)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: RAS score 9.0+ preferred. Explosive testing (vertical, broad jump) heavily weighted. Gary: 4.58 40, 38-inch vert, 120-inch broad. Van Ness: 4.58 40, 35.5-inch vert, 120-inch broad.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Explosive first step with length to convert speed to power at the point of attack. Versatility to play multiple positions along the front (stand-up, hand-in-dirt, kick inside).
most_valued_traits: Length and ability to convert speed to power
Run defense capability — must set edge
least_important_traits: Polished pass-rush repertoire — regime has shown willingness to draft raw rushers with limited moves and develop them (Gary had 10 career sacks at Michigan)
deal_breakers: One-dimensional speed-only rushers without anchor or power
Undersized or short-armed players who can't set the edge in the run game
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite RAS / elite first-step explosion can override modest production, as seen with Gary

college_production.production_floor: No hard production floor — Gary had only 9.5 sacks across 3 years at Michigan and was still taken 12th overall. Athleticism trumps production at this position.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending trajectory; Gary's sacks increased each year. Van Ness showed increasing versatility. Sorrell (2025 4th round) improved pressure rate every year.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Values production in context — a player generating pressure on a rotation with other elite athletes (Michigan, Iowa) is credited.

examples: 2019 1.12 — Rashan Gary, Michigan: 6-5, 277, 4.58 40, 33.5-inch arms, RAS 9.95. Raw athleticism bet. Only 9.5 career sacks at Michigan but was #1 national recruit.
2023 1.13 — Lukas Van Ness, Iowa: 6-5, 272, 4.58 40, 33.25-inch arms. Versatile, played inside and outside. 9 sacks in 2022.
2025 4.124 — Barryn Sorrell, Texas: DL who can play end or kick inside. 6 sacks in 2024. 28 bench press reps at combine. Improved pressure rate every year.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile offensive lineman who can play multiple positions along the line. The Packers have heavily valued OL versatility — Elgton Jenkins played every OL position, Jordan Morgan was drafted to play tackle or guard, and the 2022 class featured 3 OL (Rhyan, Tom, Walker) who all became starters. Sullivan explicitly valued 'movable pieces' to handle injury attrition.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33.5 inches for pure tackle; shorter acceptable for OL versatility picks (Morgan had questions about arm length for tackle)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Foot speed and change of direction valued. Athletic background a plus (Morgan had track-and-field background). The Packers want linemen who can pull and reach-block in LaFleur's zone-run scheme.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No 40-time threshold but must demonstrate foot quickness in kick slide

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Pass protection technique, foot speed, and ability to mirror rushers. Zone-run scheme fit — must reach-block and move laterally.
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility (can play tackle and guard)
Anchor strength to handle bull rushes
least_important_traits: Pure mauling run-blocking power — scheme is zone-based, not gap/power
deal_breakers: Stiff athletes who cannot move laterally in zone concepts
Players who can only play one position along the line
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Proven multi-position versatility can override other concerns

college_production.production_floor: Must have started at least 1.5 seasons at a Power 5 program
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers ascending play — Morgan had a rough 2021 but improved dramatically
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values competition level — SEC and Big Ten tackles weighted more

examples: 2019 2.44 — Elgton Jenkins, Mississippi State: 6-5, 310, 5.19 40. Elite versatility — started at every OL position as a pro. 2x Pro Bowler.
2024 1.25 — Jordan Morgan, Arizona: 6-5, 325. Athletic lineman with track background. Drafted as tackle with guard versatility.
2022 3.92 — Sean Rhyan, UCLA: 6-5, 321. Guard/tackle versatility.
2022 4.132 — Zach Tom, Wake Forest: 6-4, 304. Versatile, played center and tackle in college. Became starting LT.
2022 7.249 — Rasheed Walker, Penn State: 6-6, 313. Pure left tackle. Day 3 value pick who became starter.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic interior lineman with positional versatility who fits a zone-blocking scheme. The Packers consistently sought guards and centers who could move laterally, pull, and play multiple positions. Jenkins was the prototype — elite athlete at guard who could play every position.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Shuttle and 3-cone important for zone-scheme fit. Athletic baseline matters more than pure strength.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Must be able to pull and reach-block effectively

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Lateral movement ability in zone-run concepts with enough anchor to hold up in pass protection.
least_important_traits: Elite bench press strength — scheme doesn't demand power-blocking specialists
deal_breakers: Plodding, straight-line-only interior linemen who cannot execute zone concepts
Poor pass protection fundamentals

college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending play; values improvement over time
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for system — zone-scheme college linemen valued

examples: 2019 2.44 — Elgton Jenkins, Mississippi State: 6-5, 310. Elite guard who became versatile All-Pro.
2022 3.92 — Sean Rhyan, UCLA: 6-5, 321. Guard/tackle versatility. Became full-time starter by Year 3.
2024 5.163 — Jacob Monk, Duke: Center with development upside. Team captain. Versatility valued.
2024 6.202 — Travis Glover, Georgia State: OL developmental pick. Versatile with starter potential.

DT.archetype_summary: Penetrating, disruptive interior presence in a one-gap 4-3 system. The Packers under Hafley moved to a 4-3 that demands interior DL who can shoot gaps and create pressure, not two-gap space-eaters. Kenny Clark (3x Pro Bowl) was the prototype — a 3-technique who could disrupt both run and pass. Devonte Wyatt was a penetrating DT taken in round 1. The defense wants DTs who can get upfield.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness and explosion critical. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness indicators. Clark: 4.93 40, 36-inch vert. Wyatt: 4.77 40, 35.5-inch vert.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Interior penetration and disruption. One-gap aggression. Get upfield and collapse the pocket.
most_valued_traits: First-step quickness and explosion off the snap
Interior pass-rush ability (ability to win 1-on-1 vs guards)
Penetration and gap disruption in the run game
Ability to play multiple DL spots
least_important_traits: Two-gap ability — Hafley's scheme doesn't demand space-eating, hold-the-point nose tackles
deal_breakers: Slow-footed, space-eating-only DTs without pass-rush upside
Poor motor or effort concerns
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite first-step quickness for an interior lineman

college_production.production_floor: Interior disruption metrics matter — TFLs, hurries. Clark had limited sack numbers but was a dominant run-stopper.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — rotation and scheme context matters

examples: 2016 1.27 — Kenny Clark, UCLA: 6-3, 314, 4.93 40, 36-inch vert. Penetrating 3-technique. 3x Pro Bowler.
2022 1.28 — Devonte Wyatt, Georgia: 6-3, 304, 4.77 40, 35.5-inch vert. Explosive interior rusher. RAS 9.99.
2021 5.173 — T.J. Slaton, Florida: 6-4, 330. Nose tackle depth.

CB.archetype_summary: Long, fast, athletic press-man corner with elite speed and size. The Packers heavily valued physical traits at CB — Jaire Alexander (RAS 9.54) and Eric Stokes (RAS 9.38) were both first-round picks with elite measurables. Gutekunst described Josh Jackson as having 'the length we're looking for' and Eric Stokes as having 'excellent size.' Hafley's defense uses press-man corners who can play Cover-3 zone and Cover-1 man. Length and speed are non-negotiable.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31 inches — Stokes had 32.5-inch arms, Alexander had 30.5 inches but compensated with elite overall RAS
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed first. 40 time is heavily weighted. Stokes: 4.25 40. Alexander: 4.37 40. Carrington Valentine (7th round, became starter): 4.34 40. 3-cone under 7.0 preferred.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.45 or faster strongly preferred. The Packers have consistently drafted fast corners.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Press-man and pattern-match Cover-3. Hafley's scheme demands corners who can press at the line and bail into zone or stick in man.
most_valued_traits: Speed — must be able to run with NFL WRs deep
Press technique and physicality at the line
Willingness to tackle in run support
least_important_traits: Pure zone-only instincts — scheme requires press capability as foundational
deal_breakers: Slow corners who can't run (no CB slower than 4.48 drafted in early rounds)
Soft corners who avoid contact and can't press
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite speed — Stokes' 4.25 40 was a major factor in his Round 1 selection despite limited production

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor. Stokes had limited INTs at Georgia but was drafted 29th overall based on traits.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year starters from top programs
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — credits corners who played in elite conferences against top WRs

examples: 2018 1.18 — Jaire Alexander, Louisville: 5-10, 196, 4.37 40, RAS 9.54. 2x All-Pro. Press-man specialist.
2021 1.29 — Eric Stokes, Georgia: 6-1, 194, 4.25 40, 32.5-inch arms, RAS 9.38. Elite speed.
2023 7.235 — Carrington Valentine, Kentucky: 6-0, 193, 4.34 40. Late-round speed steal. Became starter.
2024 7.255 — Kalen King, Penn State: 5-11, 190. Press corner with coverage versatility.
2025 7.237 — Micah Robinson, Tulane: Day 3 CB with speed and length.

S.archetype_summary: Versatile, multi-position safety who can play single-high free safety, box safety, and slot corner. Hafley's defense demands a ranging free safety who can patrol the deep middle (like Xavier McKinney, signed to a major FA deal) and a dropdown strong safety who can play in the box and cover slots (like Javon Bullard). The Packers drafted 3 safeties in 2024, all viewed as multi-position players. Versatility is the defining trait.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and range critical for free safety role. Bullard: 4.47 40. Evan Williams: 4.57 40 but excelled in coverage. Darnell Savage: 4.36 40.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred for free safety; box/slot safety can be slightly slower if physical

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Single-high free safety in Cover-3 base with dropdown strong safety. Strong safety/nickel hybrid role is critical in Hafley's system.
most_valued_traits: Positional versatility (both safety spots and slot corner)
Range and ball-hawking ability for FS
Tackling and physicality for SS/box role
Coverage ability in man and zone
least_important_traits: Pure run-stopping ability without coverage skills — the system needs coverage-first safeties
deal_breakers: One-dimensional box safeties who can't cover
Safeties without range who can't play single-high
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Three-position versatility (both safety spots + slot corner)

college_production.production_floor: Interceptions and turnovers valued but not required. Bullard had 4 INTs in 3 seasons.

examples: 2019 1.21 — Darnell Savage, Maryland: 5-11, 198, 4.36 40. Rangy free safety.
2024 2.58 — Javon Bullard, Georgia: 5-10, 197, 4.47 40. Nickel/safety hybrid. Versatile. Nat'l champ game defensive MVP.
2024 4.111 — Evan Williams, Oregon: 6-0, 202, 4.57 40. Run-stopping safety who earned All-Rookie honors.
2024 5.169 — Kitan Oladapo, Oregon State: Safety who can play deep or in the box.

LB.archetype_summary: Rangy, athletic linebacker who can run sideline to sideline, cover in space, and play multiple LB positions. Hafley's 4-3 defense needs two ILBs who are coverage-capable and fast enough to execute pattern-match zone concepts. The Packers invested heavily in LBs: Quay Walker (R1), Edgerrin Cooper (R2, All-Rookie), Ty'Ron Hopper (R3). All were athletic, rangy prospects from top SEC programs. Cooper led all LBs in PFF grade in 2023.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and range paramount. Walker: 4.52 40, 6-4, 241. Cooper: 6-2, 230, 4.56 40 (projected). Physical testing important. Blake Martinez (R4, 2016): became league tackles leader.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Sideline-to-sideline speed with coverage ability in zone. Must be able to drop into hook/curl zones and match TEs/RBs.
most_valued_traits: Coverage ability — must handle pattern-match zone drops
least_important_traits: Blitz ability — Hafley doesn't blitz often (27th in blitz rate in 2025), relying on simulated pressures instead
deal_breakers: Slow, plodding linebackers who can't cover
Liability in pass coverage vs. TEs and RBs
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite range combined with coverage versatility (Cooper profile)

college_production.production_floor: Must show tackle production. Walker: 67 tackles in final season. Cooper: led LBs in PFF grade. Martinez: dominated tackles. Blake-level production is valued.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending preferred. Cooper's breakout was notable.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Values LBs from top programs (Georgia, Texas A&M)

examples: 2022 1.22 — Quay Walker, Georgia: 6-4, 241, 4.52 40. Rangy, athletic. National champion.
2024 2.45 — Edgerrin Cooper, Texas A&M: 6-2, 230. Led LBs in PFF grade. All-Rookie team. Elite all-around LB.
2024 3.91 — Ty'Ron Hopper, Missouri: Athletic, physical. Can play edge or inside. Versatile.
2016 4.131 — Blake Martinez, Stanford: 6-2, 237. Led NFL in tackles. High-floor producer.

WR.archetype_summary: Big, fast, versatile playmaker. The Packers historically waited on WRs (no first-round WR from 2002-2024) but still found elite producers: Christian Watson (R2, 6-4, 208, 4.36), Jayden Reed (R2, 5-11, 187, 4.45), Romeo Doubs (R4, 6-2, 203). In 2025, they broke the drought with Matthew Golden (R1, 4.29 40) and Savion Williams (R3, 6-5, 220, versatile). The pattern: speed is non-negotiable, but they want different body types in the WR room. They love big-bodied WRs with speed and contested-catch ability.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40 time heavily valued. Watson: 4.36. Golden: 4.29. Reed: 4.45. Doubs: 4.44. Speed is the common thread.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster — every WR drafted in early rounds ran 4.50 or faster

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Speed to threaten vertically, with enough size/physicality to win contested catches. Versatility to play X, Z, or slot.
most_valued_traits: Versatility — ability to play multiple WR positions
least_important_traits: Polished route running as a standalone trait — regime has shown willingness to draft raw athletes and develop routes
deal_breakers: Slow WRs who can't threaten vertically
Weak contested-catch ability with small frames
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Sub-4.35 speed combined with size (Watson, Golden profiles)

college_production.production_floor: Watson had just 26 catches in his final college season but had elite speed. Doubs had 72 catches. Golden had 58 catches and 9 TDs. No hard floor when traits are elite.
college_production.production_trajectory: No strong preference — Watson was a breakout, Reed was a steady producer
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for opportunity — Watson's limited production at NDSU was contextual

examples: 2022 2.34 — Christian Watson, NDSU: 6-4, 208, 4.36 40. Big-bodied deep threat. Traded up to get him.
2023 2.50 — Jayden Reed, Michigan State: 5-11, 187, 4.45 40. Versatile slot/returner.
2022 4.132 — Romeo Doubs, Nevada: 6-2, 203, 4.44 40. Contested-catch ability. Breakout player.
2025 1.23 — Matthew Golden, Texas: 4.29 40. 58 catches, 9 TDs, elite deep ball.
2025 3.87 — Savion Williams, TCU: 6-5, 220. Versatile — WR/RB/returner. 12 total TDs.
2023 5.159 — Dontayvion Wicks, Virginia: 6-1, 206. Contested-catch specialist.

TE.archetype_summary: Receiving-capable athletic TE who can flex out and create mismatches, while being willing to block in the run game. Tucker Kraft (R3, 2023) is the prototype — athletic enough to be a receiving weapon (81 catches in first two seasons) while tough enough to block in LaFleur's offense. The Packers want TEs who can do both.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness indicators. Speed in the 4.55-4.70 range acceptable if athletic otherwise.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Hybrid receiving/blocking TE. Must be able to line up inline and block effectively, then flex out and create mismatches in the passing game.
least_important_traits: Pure blocking without receiving upside — regime wants both
deal_breakers: Receiving-only TEs who cannot or will not block
Stiff athletes without separation ability
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite receiving ability combined with blocking competence

college_production.production_floor: Kraft had 56 catches, 618 yards, 6 TDs in final college season
college_production.context_sensitivity: Adjusts for usage — TE production in run-heavy offenses is weighted differently

examples: 2023 3.78 — Tucker Kraft, South Dakota State: 6-5, 254, 4.69 40. 56 catches for 618 yards, 6 TDs in final season. Physical, athletic hybrid TE. Became leading TE (81 catches in first 2 years).
2023 4.116 — Luke Musgrave, Oregon State: 6-6, 253, 4.61 40. Athletic receiving TE. Starter as rookie.

RB.archetype_summary: Explosive, versatile back with breakaway speed and receiving ability who can complement a thunder back. The Packers have valued RBs who can create big plays — Aaron Jones (R5, 2017) led the league in TD rushing and yards per carry. MarShawn Lloyd (R3, 2024) was drafted for his explosiveness (7.1 YPC). AJ Dillon (R2, 2020) was the power complement. They don't invest premium capital at RB but seek explosive, dynamic backs in rounds 3-5.

physical_profile.weight_range: 200-230 (wide range — seeks different profiles for different roles)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and explosiveness. Jones: 4.56 40 but 31-inch vert, 117-inch broad. Lloyd: 4.46 40. Dillon: 4.53 40 at 247 lbs.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred for primary back

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Explosive, big-play ability with receiving capability out of the backfield. Must fit zone-run concepts.
most_valued_traits: Explosive play ability and breakaway speed
Vision and lateral agility in zone-run scheme
least_important_traits: Pass protection — valued but not a primary draft criterion at RB
deal_breakers: One-dimensional early-down-only backs without receiving ability
Ball security concerns are real issues (Lloyd's fumbles were flagged)

college_production.production_floor: No hard floor — Jones had 1,773 yards as a senior but was a 5th-round pick. Values YPC over volume.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values explosive metrics — YPC, broken tackles, big plays — over volume
college_production.context_sensitivity: Context-adjusted — OL quality and scheme matter

examples: 2017 5.182 — Aaron Jones, UTEP: 5-9, 208, 4.56 40. Led NFL in TDs in 2019. Pro Bowler.
2020 2.62 — AJ Dillon, Boston College: 6-0, 247, 4.53 40. Power complement. Huge runner.
2024 3.88 — MarShawn Lloyd, USC: 5-9, 220, 4.46 40. Explosive, 7.1 YPC. Contact balance.

QB.archetype_summary: The Green Bay Packers have a long tradition of drafting QBs before you need them — Rodgers behind Favre, Love behind Rodgers. Sullivan explicitly referenced this philosophy: 'If you can help it, don't wait till you don't have a quarterback to find one.' Jordan Love was traded up for at pick 26 in 2020. Love is a dual-threat with a strong arm, 6-4, 224, 4.74 40. The Packers also regularly draft developmental QBs on Day 3 (Sean Clifford R5 2023, Michael Pratt R7 2024). Sullivan will need a QB in Miami but his approach is likely patience — draft for long-term rather than panic.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength valued highly. Mobility/athleticism is a plus but not primary. Love: 4.74 40. The Packers have valued QBs with arm talent and enough mobility to extend plays.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No specific requirement but athleticism is valued

playing_style.primary_style: Arm talent and pocket presence with enough mobility to extend and create. Not a run-first QB.
most_valued_traits: Pocket presence and ability to extend plays
least_important_traits: Designed rushing production — not seeking a run-first QB
deal_breakers: Limited arm strength
Poor decision-making and turnover-prone play
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite arm talent combined with mobility — Love was selected despite Aaron Rodgers still being on the roster

college_production.production_floor: Love: 32 TDs in 2018 but dropped to 20 TDs, 17 INTs in 2019. Production decline didn't deter the pick — projection mattered.
college_production.production_trajectory: Flash seasons valued even if production declined (Love's 2018)
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — Love's regression was attributed to terrible supporting cast at Utah State

examples: 2020 1.26 — Jordan Love, Utah State: 6-4, 224, 4.74 40. Traded up to select. Elite arm talent. Production declined in final year but traits were exceptional.
2023 5.149 — Sean Clifford, Penn State: Experienced, high-floor backup.
2024 7.245 — Michael Pratt, Tulane: Developmental Day 3 QB.

QB.confidence_note: Sullivan's QB approach for Miami is the biggest unknown. He has explicitly said 'it starts with the QB' and is reportedly pursuing Malik Willis (former Packer). The Dolphins' 2026 QB situation is in flux with Tua Tagovailoa's future uncertain.

reach_tolerance.notes: Packers under Gutekunst/Sullivan were willing to reach for athletes with elite physical traits. Rashan Gary (12th overall) was seen as a slight reach given his modest Michigan production. Jordan Love (26th, traded up from 30) was a controversial pick with Aaron Rodgers still on the roster. Quay Walker (22nd) was considered a positional reach by some. The regime has shown willingness to defy consensus when they're convinced of a player's ceiling.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Expected to run a zone-based run scheme (Sullivan: 'build from the inside out,' values OL who can pull and reach-block). OC Bobby Slowik hired — he comes from the Texans/49ers coaching tree, expected to run a Shanahan-influenced outside zone offense. This means versatile, athletic OL who can move laterally, and RBs who can make one cut and go.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: 4-3 single-high safety defense with Cover-3 as base coverage. Hafley's scheme: 4-down wide-9 front, penetrating DL, two ILBs who can cover, press-man corners who bail into zone, single-high free safety. Heavy use of simulated pressures (show 5-6 rushers, drop out to 4-man rush). Low blitz rate. Requires: fast CBs with press ability, ranging FS, versatile SS/nickel, penetrating DTs, athletic ILBs, and 4-3 DEs who can rush and set the edge.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Complete regime change from Mike McDaniel's speed-based offense and Anthony Weaver's defense. Hafley's 4-3 single-high is a significant departure from what Miami has run. This is Hafley's first year as HC and Sullivan's first draft — scheme fit demands will heavily influence early picks. DC Sean Duggan comes from Green Bay's system, ensuring continuity with Hafley's defensive vision.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate risk tolerance inherited from the Packers tree. Gutekunst/Sullivan made bold moves (trading up for Love, drafting Gary over more proven options, trading for Micah Parsons) but also invested heavily in high-floor players (multiple team captains drafted, character-first approach). Sullivan will likely balance upside bets with safe, high-character picks. In a rebuild year, he may lean slightly more aggressive to find foundational pieces.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Low-to-moderate recency bias expected. Sullivan comes from an organization that drafted methodically regardless of season results. The Packers drafted Love behind Rodgers despite back-to-back 13-win seasons. Sullivan has stated he will build 'with discipline' and 'intentionally.' However, Miami's extensive needs at CB, OL, WR, and DL will naturally push early picks toward those areas.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Sullivan has already shown willingness to cut ties — he released Tyreek Hill, Bradley Chubb, and Austin Jackson (Grier draft picks) early in his tenure, signaling no attachment to previous regime's investments.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Above-average combine weight. The Packers under Gutekunst/Sullivan were described as 'treasuring RAS' (Relative Athletic Score). 7 of 9 first-round picks had RAS of 9.0+. However, Sullivan at the combine stated: 'I'm looking for good football players; I'm not looking for guys that are particularly good testers but don't show up on tape.' This suggests he uses measurables as confirming data, not primary data. Film first, but athletic thresholds matter.
psychological_profile.character_description: Very high character standards. Sullivan repeatedly emphasized 'mental toughness, physical toughness, burning desire to win.' He described wanting 'we over me' players. The Packers under this regime drafted numerous team captains (Morgan, Hopper, Williams, Monk, Oladapo were all captains). Sullivan's emphasis on culture — 'when you draft and develop and you raise your own, they're bought in' — suggests character is a primary filter, not a tiebreaker.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Sullivan explicitly said 'It starts with the QB' and referenced the Packers tradition of drafting QBs before you need them (Rodgers behind Favre, Love behind Rodgers): 'If you can help it, don't wait till you don't have a quarterback to find one.' He appears likely to pursue Malik Willis in free agency (former Packer, connection to Jon Robinson) but will also look to draft a developmental QB if the right one is available, even if he signs a bridge starter.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Balanced but leans slightly toward projection/traits when the athletic profile is exceptional. Rashan Gary: elite traits, modest production — drafted 12th. Jordan Love: production declined in final year — traded up to draft him. Christian Watson: 26 catches in final year at NDSU — drafted 34th. The regime will override production concerns for elite athletes, but not for average athletes. Sullivan at the combine: 'Are they productive football players on film?... I'm looking for good football players.'

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No hard floor when speed is elite (Watson: 26 catches). With average speed, 50+ catches expected.
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: No hard floor — values YPC and explosive plays over volume
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: No hard floor — Gary had 9.5 career sacks at Michigan and was still picked 12th overall
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear threshold — traits override stats consistently
production_thresholds_by_position.QB: Must have started at least 1 full season
production_thresholds_by_position.LB: Tackle production expected — Walker: 67, Cooper: elite PFF grade
production_thresholds_by_position.OL: Must have started at least 1.5 seasons at a Power 5 program

college_production_preferences.notes: The Packers/Sullivan regime is more of a traits-first operation than a production-first one. The quote from the combine is revealing: 'Obviously, you want big guys that are good athletes, but at the end of the day, it comes back to how do they play football.' Translation: athleticism is the screening filter, but film is the decision-maker. Production is checked but can be overridden by elite physical tools.

CB.notes: Alexander 4.37, Stokes 4.25, Valentine 4.34 — regime consistently drafts fast CBs

EDGE.notes: Gary RAS 9.95, Van Ness strong measurables

DT.notes: Clark 4.93/36-inch vert, Wyatt 4.77/35.5-inch vert — explosion matters

S.notes: Savage 4.36, Bullard 4.47 — speed valued for single-high role

LB.notes: Walker 4.52, Cooper athletic — range is critical

OT.notes: Values athletic profile for zone scheme but no hard 40 cutoff

RB.notes: Values explosion more than straight-line speed

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences with a hard cutoff at CB (speed) and EDGE (length/RAS). Other positions have strong preferences that have been violated for exceptional film.

size_preferences_by_position.LB: 225-250, with emphasis on range and speed
size_preferences_by_position.CB: Prefers 5-10+ but will draft 5-9 if speed is elite (Alexander 5-10)
size_preferences_by_position.WR: Loves big-bodied WRs (Watson 6-4, Williams 6-5) but also drafts slots (Reed 5-11)

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: RAS (Relative Athletic Score) is a known Packers favorite. 7 of 9 Gutekunst first-round picks had RAS 9.0+. Vertical jump and broad jump as explosion indicators. 40-yard dash for CBs and WRs specifically. 3-cone for EDGE evaluation.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Sullivan at the 2026 combine: 'First and foremost, it comes back to the tape... I'm not looking for guys that are particularly good testers but don't show up on tape.' Film-first stated philosophy, but the Packers track record shows elite measurables can override tape concerns (Gary, Watson, Stokes all had questions about production but had elite traits). Best description: film is the primary evaluator, measurables are confirming data AND eliminators at certain thresholds.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions (EDGE, CB, OT, DT, QB), high-ceiling athletes with RAS 9.0+. Sullivan stated 'build from the inside out' suggesting OL/DL lean. Willing to trade up if a target is available (Gutekunst traded up 4 of 8 first rounds). In a rebuild with pick 11, likely to take the best player available at a premium position. Cornerback, offensive tackle, or EDGE most likely based on need and value intersection.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Shift toward filling scheme-critical needs. The Packers frequently addressed needs here — Elgton Jenkins R2, Javon Bullard R2, Edgerrin Cooper R2. Day 2 is where scheme fit becomes critical. Expect positions that directly serve Hafley's defense (LB, S, DL) and Slowik's offense (IOL, WR, TE). Willing to trade within Day 2 to accumulate picks (Packers traded back multiple times in rounds 2-3).
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, positional versatility pieces, and special teams contributors. The Packers found significant value on Day 3: Aaron Jones (R5), Romeo Doubs (R4), Carrington Valentine (R7), Evan Williams (R4). Sullivan will target competitive, high-character players who can contribute on special teams while developing. Multiple Day 3 picks expected to be allocated to OL depth, developmental QB, and backup DB.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: The Packers under Gutekunst were historically cognizant of comp pick implications in FA decisions. Sullivan likely carries this forward — moderate FA signings to preserve comp picks.

EDGE.note: Gary became productive but ACL-plagued; Van Ness has underperformed. Mixed but high investment.

CB.note: Alexander: 2x All-Pro. Valentine: became starter from R7. Stokes: bust. King: TBD. Strong but not flawless.

OL.note: Jenkins: Pro Bowler. Tom, Rhyan, Walker: all starters. Morgan: TBD after injury. Strong OL evaluation.

LB.note: Cooper: All-Rookie. Walker: inconsistent. Martinez: hit (pre-Gutekunst era but Sullivan era). Hopper: TBD.

S.note: Savage: bust. Williams: All-Rookie. Bullard: solid starter. Oladapo: TBD. Mixed but improving.

WR.note: Watson: when healthy, excellent. Reed: strong. Doubs: solid. Wicks: inconsistent. Golden: TBD. Rodgers: bust.

DT.note: Wyatt: developing. Clark: 3x Pro Bowl (Sullivan era scout). Strong when they invest early.

QB.note: Love: franchise QB. Clifford and Pratt: backups as intended.

RB.note: Jones: Pro Bowler. Dillon: productive. Lloyd: disappointing rookie year.

blind_spots: Safety evaluation has been inconsistent — Savage was a first-round bust. First-round EDGE investment has not yet produced an elite pass rusher (Gary pre-ACL was approaching it). LB evaluation improving but Walker was a reach.
strengths: OL evaluation is elite — consistently finds starters and multi-position players across all rounds. CB evaluation at its best (Alexander, Valentine) is exceptional. WR evaluation on Day 2 has been strong (Watson, Reed, Doubs). DT evaluation when investing early is strong (Clark).

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Very conservative spender. Sullivan explicitly called free agency 'dangerous waters' and said 'We don't want a team full of mercenaries.' The Packers under Thompson/Gutekunst were historically among the most draft-centric teams in the NFL. Gutekunst opened up slightly (McKinney $67M, Josh Jacobs), but Sullivan's language signals a return to Thompson-era conservatism. Expect Miami to be extremely restrained in FA — value signings, second-wave acquisitions, and role players only.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When the Packers signed a top FA (McKinney at S in 2024), they STILL drafted 3 safeties that same draft. The Packers' philosophy is that depth and competition are paramount — they don't stop drafting a position just because they signed a starter there. Sullivan: 'Competition drives the individual to excellence.' Expect the Dolphins to draft at positions of need regardless of FA additions.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at every position. Supplement through trades for proven veterans only when the price is right. Use free agency for role players and targeted additions, not franchise-building. Sullivan's stated vision aligns perfectly with the Thompson/Gutekunst model: draft young, develop them, retain your own, and avoid being held hostage by the FA market. Miami in 2026 will be heavily draft-dependent given limited cap space (~$4M before cuts) and the need for a complete rebuild.

notable_patterns: Sullivan has 0 drafts as GM. All patterns are inferred from his 22-year tenure as a key decision-maker in Green Bay.
Packers under Gutekunst/Sullivan (2018-2025): Defense-heavy in Round 1 (7 of 9 picks defensive), strong RAS preference (7 of 9 picks RAS 9.0+), young prospects (8 of 9 picks age 22 or younger).
Traded up in Round 1 four times (2018, 2019, 2020, 2023), suggesting moderate trade-up willingness when a target is available.
Positional versatility is the single most frequently cited trait in post-draft pressers — 'versatile' or 'versatility' mentioned for 27+ draft picks.
Sullivan personally emphasized 'we've invested in guys made of the right stuff' and finding players who respond to 'high-level competition.'

biggest_reaches.context: Traded up from 30 to 26 to draft backup QB behind Aaron Rodgers. Extremely controversial. Has since become franchise QB.
Elite athlete (RAS 9.95, #1 recruit) but modest production (9.5 sacks in 3 years at Michigan). Traits-over-production bet.

biggest_steals.context: Earned PFWA All-Rookie honors. Became key starter. Excellent Day 3 find.
4.34 40 speed. Became starting CB as a 7th-round pick.
Led NFL in rushing TDs in 2019. Pro Bowler. Elite Day 3 find.

confidence_notes: Zero drafts as Dolphins GM. Profile is built almost entirely from Sullivan's 22-year tenure at Green Bay (Tier 1 evidence: Packers draft picks he helped select; Tier 1 evidence: his public statements as Dolphins GM at combine and introductory presser). Secondary inputs include Hafley's scheme requirements and Kyle Smith's scouting background. The Packers scouting tree is one of the most well-documented in the NFL, which increases confidence in the lineage-based predictions. However, Sullivan's specific decision-making as the final authority is untested. Profile should be substantially refreshed after the 2026 draft and again after the 2027 draft. Key unknowns: (1) How much does Sullivan diverge from Gutekunst? (2) How much influence does Kyle Smith have on the board? (3) Does Sullivan's OL/DL emphasis override BPA at pick 11?
